resources.string: 🇪🇸 30 Days of Spanish Topics 🇵🇷
Gender of Nouns
Numbers: 1–10
Plural Forms of Nouns
Definite and Indefinite Articles
The Verb Form \"Hay\"
Subject Pronouns
Regular Verbs
Adjectives
Days of the Week
Numbers: 11–30
Ser and Estar
Negation
Questions
Possessive Adjectives
Tener, venir
\"Tener Que\" and \"Hay Que\"
Expressions with \"Tener\"
Weather Expressions
The Personal \"a\"
Contractions
Stem-Changing Verbs
Estar, Ir, Dar
\"Ir A\" + Infinitive
\"Acabar De\" + Infinitive
\"Volver A\" + Infinitive
Ordinal Numbers
Months, Seasons, and Dates
Comparisons of Inequality and Equality
Superlatives
Pronouns as Objects of Prepositions
Learn how Spanish nouns are classified as either masculine or feminine, and the general rules for identifying their gender.
Master the Spanish words for numbers one through ten, building the foundation for counting and basic arithmetic.
Discover the rules for making Spanish nouns plural, including adding -s or -es and the corresponding spelling changes.
Understand how to use \"the\" (el, la, los, las) and \"a/an/some\" (un, una, unos, unas) correctly with nouns based on gender and number.
Learn to use the versatile word \"hay,\" which means both \"there is\" and \"there are,\" to talk about existence.
Explore the Spanish subject pronouns such as yo, tú, él, ella, and usted, which indicate who is performing the action.
Master the conjugation patterns for regular -ar, -er, and -ir verbs in the present tense to express ongoing actions.
Learn how to use descriptive words in Spanish, including their agreement in gender and number with the nouns they modify.
Memorize the Spanish names for the days of the week and learn how to talk about schedules and routines.
Continue building your number skills by learning to count from eleven to thirty in Spanish.
Understand the crucial difference between the two Spanish verbs meaning \"to be,\" using ser for permanent qualities and estar for temporary states.
Learn how to form negative sentences in Spanish by placing \"no\" and other negative words before the verb.
Discover how to ask questions in Spanish using intonation, question words like qué, quién, and dónde, and inverted question marks.
Master possessive adjectives like mi, tu, and su to express ownership and relationships, ensuring they agree in number with the nouns they describe.
Conjugate and use the important irregular verbs tener (to have) and venir (to come) in various contexts.
Learn two common Spanish constructions for expressing obligation: \"tener que\" (to have to) and \"hay que\" (one must).
Explore useful idiomatic expressions with the verb tener, such as tener hambre (to be hungry) and tener sueño (to be sleepy).
Acquire the vocabulary and phrases needed to describe the weather, asking \"¿Qué tiempo hace?\" and responding appropriately.
Understand the rule for using the preposition \"a\" before a direct object that refers to a specific person or pet.
Learn how the prepositions \"a\" and \"de\" combine with the masculine article \"el\" to form the contractions \"al\" and \"del.\"
Master the patterns of stem-changing verbs (o:ue, e:ie, e:i), where the vowel in the stem changes in all forms except nosotros and vosotros.
Conjugate three highly irregular verbs—estar (to be), ir (to go), and dar (to give)—and learn their common uses.
Learn to talk about future plans and actions using the simple and common construction \"ir a\" + infinitive, meaning \"going to.\"
Discover how to use the phrase \"acabar de\" + infinitive to say that someone has just done something.
Learn to express the repetition of an action using the construction \"volver a\" + infinitive, meaning \"to do something again.\"
Master ordinal numbers like primero (first), segundo (second), and tercero (third) to establish order and sequence.
Learn the Spanish names for months and seasons, and practice forming and expressing dates correctly.
Discover how to make comparisons of inequality in Spanish using \"más…que\" (more…than) and \"menos…que\" (less…than).
Learn to form comparisons of equality using \"tan…como\" (as…as) for adjectives and adverbs, and \"tanto…como\" for nouns.
Master superlative constructions to express the highest or lowest degree, such as \"el más inteligente\" (the most intelligent) and \"el mejor\" (the best).
